entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Useful lower-priced Austrian anchor against single-vineyard Gruner or other cool-climate dry whites.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Pairs cleanly with Brundlmayer Terrassen for a Gruner quality and site hierarchy flight.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Adds high-acid Atlantic white diversity without reusing Loire Chenin.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Good high-quality white comparator where price/quality is high without relying on Burgundy or Loire.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Useful for winemaking-diversity questions because it gives an oak-aged white contrast.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2023 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Adds clear method diversity versus steel-aged aromatic whites.

entries.producer: R. Lopez de Heredia Vina Tondonia
entries.vintage_guidance: Use mature release; 2014 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Strong Paper 3 or Paper 1 method contrast; do not confuse with fresh Rioja or Australian Riesling.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use mature released vintage; 2016 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Good non-sparkling Paper 3 method wine; keep distinct from fortified oxidative wines.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Useful for style/variety contrast; not a high-quality ladder endpoint.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Useful alternative to Loire Chenin for blend logic and texture.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current, recent, or library release; 2018 library was researched.
entries.selection_notes: High-value exam wine because quality comes from site/acid/ageing rather than oak.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2020 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Pairs with Prado Enea for clear Reserva vs Gran Reserva and price/quality ladder.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use mature current release; 2016 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: High-quality maturity endpoint; avoid overusing Rioja as the default red ladder.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2021 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Good way to test dry Douro blend logic without defaulting to Port.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2022 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Best used against Vale Meao to prevent one-dimensional Douro assumptions.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2022 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Good if the flight needs a distinct variety and commercial-recognition angle.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2022 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Useful icon wine, but keep on cooldown if Sadie/Palladius has appeared recently.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2021 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Adds underused regional diversity for Paper 2 without relying on Bordeaux/Piedmont.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2020 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Good Nebbiolo-like structural contrast while avoiding actual Piedmont repetition.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2021 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Use for quality/hierarchy flights; avoid if the mock already uses Bordeaux Cabernet heavily.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2021 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Strong contrast with Don Melchor or Purple Angel for Chilean red style/hierarchy.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2021 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Quality endpoint for Malbec; pair with less expensive Mendoza examples if needed.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2022 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Good blend-logic wine and more MW-plausible than generic high-alcohol Zin.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2021 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Useful for blend logic and commercial-quality questions.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current release; 2019 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: High-quality Shiraz endpoint; useful alternative to Barossa stereotypes.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2022 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Adds Paper 3 fortified method diversity without reusing Madeira or Port.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2022 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Useful paired with Maury or Setubal for method and price/quality contrast.

entries.vintage_guidance: NV 20-year designation.
entries.selection_notes: Strong Paper 3 alternative to Madeira/Sherry with clear sourceable facts.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent release; 2018 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Useful for method/style differentiation; should be source-checked for exact vintage details before final mock use.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent release; 2021 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: High-quality sweet wine alternative to Sauternes/Tokaji.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent release; 2022 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Excellent method-mechanism contrast against botrytised, fortified, or passito wines.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent release; 2022 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Good sweet-wine method alternative; avoids overusing Tokaji or Sauternes.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use a current or recent vintage; 2010 was researched historically.
entries.selection_notes: Useful mainstream white anchor that is recognisable but not expensive.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2008 was researched historically.
entries.selection_notes: A classic lower-cost white with enough identity to support contrast flights.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use the current release or the researched 2014 historical bottling.
entries.selection_notes: Exactly the sort of commercial foil needed to keep quality flights from becoming all icons.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2007 was researched historically.
entries.selection_notes: Better than an icon for building an affordable red ladder.

entries.vintage_guidance: NV
entries.selection_notes: A straightforward commercial Champagne that can anchor sparkling comparisons.

entries.vintage_guidance: NV
entries.selection_notes: Gives the bank a simple, familiar sparkling style with clear market positioning.

entries.vintage_guidance: NV
entries.selection_notes: Very useful for proving sparkling need not mean Champagne.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; historical researched bottling had source-needed vintage.
entries.selection_notes: Excellent non-Champagne sparkling alternative with a clear commercial role.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2017 was researched historically.
entries.selection_notes: Gives a sweet sparkling alternative that is very different from Champagne or Prosecco.

entries.vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2020–2023 widely available; 2020/2022 researched. Estate is the largest 1er+Grand Cru holder in Chablis; acquired by Domaines Barons de Rothschild Lafite in 2024.
entries.selection_notes: Reference Chablis 1er Cru in steely, mineral, low-new-oak style — pairs as the cool-Burgundy foil to oak-driven Côte de Beaune Chardonnay (Meursault/Puligny) and to riper New World Chardonnays (Sonoma/Margaret River). Useful as the benchmark in any single-variety Chardonnay quality or origin flight.

entries.vintage_guidance: Released annually; 2019/2022 researched. A flagship southern-Burgundy Chardonnay and the largest-volume Mâcon-Lugny on world markets.
entries.selection_notes: Inexpensive, ripe-ish southern Burgundy Chardonnay anchor. Useful as the value-tier foil in any Chardonnay quality ladder or Burgundy regional spread (Mâcon → Chablis → Côte de Beaune village). Provides the unoaked / minimally oaked end of the Burgundy spectrum without going to Chablis.

entries.vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2021–2024 in market; 2024 researched. Village-level cuvée drawing from across the appellation's 26 climats.
entries.selection_notes: Côte de Beaune village white anchor in the textbook négociant-domaine style — barrel-fermented and barrel-aged with modest new oak. Sits between Chablis (steely, low oak) and the Mâconnais (ripe, unoaked) inside a Burgundy white quality ladder, and contrasts oak-driven New World Chardonnays in cross-country flights.

entries.vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2021–2023 in market; 2022/2023 researched. Old-vine village cuvée from 9.82 ha across ~20 plots planted 1945–1969.
entries.selection_notes: Côte de Nuits village Pinot Noir benchmark in a powerful, structured Gevrey style. Anchors any Pinot Noir cross-country flight (Burgundy vs Oregon vs Central Otago) and the Burgundy red side of any quality ladder. The Vieilles Vignes selection makes it punch above a basic village in concentration without crossing into 1er Cru pricing.

entries.vintage_guidance: Annual release; 2021/2022/2024 researched. Estate cuvée from the Drouhin family's Dundee Hills vineyard, made in the same Joseph Drouhin Burgundian style since 1988.
entries.selection_notes: Oregon Pinot anchor in the most overtly Burgundian Willamette house style — useful precisely because it can be confused with village Burgundy in a blind. Pairs in cross-country Pinot flights against Gevrey-Chambertin (Old World, more iron/earth) and Central Otago (riper, more concentrated New World). Also a strong choice for a 'guess the country' anti-overlink question.

entries.vintage_guidance: Annual release; 2021–2024 vintages researched. Blend of Felton Road's four Bannockburn estate vineyards (Elms, Macmuir, Calvert, Cornish Point); fully Demeter biodynamic since 2010.
entries.selection_notes: Reference Central Otago Pinot at benchmark quality. Riper, more sun-saturated than Burgundy or Oregon while still showing fine tannins and savoury complexity from old-clone biodynamic estate fruit. Use as the warmest-climate end of any Pinot Noir cross-country flight (Burgundy → Oregon → Central Otago) and as the New World benchmark to test whether a candidate pushes too quickly to California or Australia.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2019 (70% Cabernet Sauvignon, 24% Merlot, 3% Cabernet Franc, 3% Petit Verdot; aged 75% new French oak) and 2020 (60% CS, 31% M, 5% PV, 4% CF) were researched.
entries.selection_notes: Use as left-bank Cabernet benchmark in cross-origin Cabernet flights against Napa or Coonawarra, or as the high point of a Medoc hierarchy flight against a Cru Bourgeois.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2019 (74% Merlot, 26% Cabernet Franc; 18 months French oak, 50% new) and 2022 were researched. Estate composition is roughly 70% Merlot / 30% Cabernet Franc on the limestone plateau.
entries.selection_notes: Right-bank Merlot-led counterpoint to Medoc Cabernet for Bordeaux blend-logic and bank discrimination questions; limestone chalkiness is the diagnostic regional marker.

entries.wine_name: Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte Rouge Grand Cru Classe
entries.vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2016 (65% Cabernet Sauvignon, 30% Merlot, 4% Cabernet Franc, 1% Petit Verdot; 18 months oak, 60% new from in-house cooperage) and 2022 (64% CS, 32% M, 3% CF, 1% PV) were researched.
entries.selection_notes: Adds a Graves-style Cabernet-led blend variation against Medoc Pauillac for sub-regional discrimination on the left bank; smoky/graphite Graves character is the diagnostic shift versus Pauillac cassis-and-cedar.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2019 (65% Merlot, 29% Cabernet Sauvignon, 3% Cabernet Franc, 3% Petit Verdot; 14.5% abv; 12 months ageing, 50% one-year-old French oak / 50% tank) was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Value-tier Medoc to use as the low end of a Bordeaux quality ladder against Lynch-Bages, or to test whether candidates can place a Bordeaux blend at mainstream price level without classed-growth concentration.

entries.wine_name: Heitz Cellar Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
entries.vintage_guidance: Use recent release; 2018 (100% Cabernet Sauvignon, 13.8% abv; aged two years in a combination of large French oak casks and barriques, then one year in bottle before release) was researched.
entries.sub_region: Napa Valley (multi-sub-AVA: Oakville, Rutherford, Howell Mountain, St. Helena, Oak Knoll, Calistoga)
entries.selection_notes: Restrained, classically built Napa Cab useful in cross-origin Cabernet flights against Lynch-Bages or Wynns; lower alcohol and fresher profile than Caymus-style options, better-suited to variety identification questions where ripeness alone should not give away origin.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2019 (100% Cabernet Sauvignon, 13.8% abv, 14 months in new (31%) and seasoned French hogsheads (61%), barriques (26%) and vats (13%)) and 2022 (96-97 pt) were researched.
entries.selection_notes: Coonawarra's benchmark Cab; pairs against Napa Cab and Bordeaux left-bank Cab for same-variety cross-origin flights. Mint/eucalypt and terra rossa-driven freshness are the diagnostic regional markers.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2023/2024 widely available. 2017 cited for detailed analytical (47.2 g/L RS, 9.6 g/L TA, 8% abv); 2024 is regarded as a classic Kabinett year.
entries.selection_notes: Benchmark Mosel Kabinett: ~8% abv, 35-45 g/L RS, racy slate-driven acid. Use as the off-dry / fruity Mosel anchor in any Riesling sweetness ladder (Kabinett -> Spätlese -> Auslese) or in a cross-country dry-vs-off-dry Riesling spread (vs Alsace Frédéric Émile or Clare Polish Hill). ID deliberately distinct from existing Loosen Sekt entry.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2023 and 2024 widely circulating. JJ Prüm publishes no formal tech sheets, only vintage reports — research the specific vintage via the estate's vintage report and importer notes.
entries.rs_level: sweet (~60-90 g/L typical Prüm Spätlese band; not formally disclosed by estate)
entries.selection_notes: Iconic single-vineyard Spätlese from Mosel's most celebrated producer. Deep grey weathered Devonian slate, 70% slope, SSW exposure, 5 ha estate holding. Use as the upper rung of a Mosel sweetness ladder (Kabinett -> Spätlese -> Auslese) or as a sugar-acid-balance trap question — looks sweet on the nose, but acid + alcohol balance hides RS. Pairs with Loosen Kabinett to create a single-village, single-variety, ascending Prädikat flight.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; producer PDF expertise sheets are published per vintage. Recent vintages 2021, 2022, 2023 widely available.
entries.classification: VDP.Grosse Lage — Grosses Gewächs (dry Erste Lage equivalent)
entries.selection_notes: Iconic single-vineyard dry Rheingau Riesling from steep SW-facing phyllite/loess slope. Use as the dry German Riesling anchor against off-dry Mosel Kabinett (same variety, opposite RS) or against Trimbach Frédéric Émile (dry Riesling cross-country, Germany vs France) or Grosset Polish Hill (Old World vs New World dry Riesling). Highlights the VDP GG quality tier vs Prädikat ladder distinction.

entries.vintage_guidance: Released ~7 years post-vintage. 2015, 2017, 2018, 2019 widely circulating. 2017 importer tech sheet: 13.5% abv, RS 2.4 g/L, TA 7.64 g/L, pH 3.08.
entries.classification: Blended from Grand Cru Geisberg and Grand Cru Osterberg sites (historically labelled Alsace AOC, not Grand Cru on the bottle)
entries.rs_level: dry (2-3 g/L typical; 2.4 g/L on 2017 tech sheet)
entries.selection_notes: Reference dry Alsace Riesling — higher abv (13.5%), bone-dry, no MLF, muschelkalk minerality and developed kerosene/smoke with age. Use as the Alsace anchor in a cross-country dry Riesling flight (Rheingau GG / Alsace FE / Clare Polish Hill / Eden Valley Pewsey Vale). Sets up the classic Alsace vs Mosel sweetness-and-alcohol contrast.

entries.vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2022, 2023, 2024 widely circulating. 2019 producer tech sheet: 12.5% abv, RS 1.9 g/L, pH 2.97, TA 7.3 g/L. 2022 abv 12.9%; 2023 12.9%; 2024 12.1%.
entries.classification: Langton's Classification: Exceptional. ACO-certified organic and biodynamic estate vineyard.
entries.rs_level: bone_dry (1-2 g/L; 1.9 g/L on 2019 tech sheet)
entries.selection_notes: Australia's iconic dry Riesling — bone-dry, screwcap, blue-slate-driven Polish Hill sub-region with very high acid and low pH. Use as the New World anchor in a global dry Riesling spread. Powerful contrast vs Mosel off-dry slate (same blue slate motif, opposite RS) and vs Rheingau GG (both dry, but Rheingau is fuller-bodied on phyllite vs Clare's lean structure). A classic 'Old vs New World same-variety' question piece.

entries.vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2023, 2024, 2025 widely available. Importer tech sheet (2024): 11.5% abv, RS 1.20 g/L, TA 6.80 g/L, pH 3.1. Winemaker Louisa Rose.
entries.classification: Sustainable Winegrowing Australia certified. First Riesling vineyard planted in Eden Valley (1847).
entries.selection_notes: Mainstream-priced Eden Valley dry Riesling — historically the first Riesling vineyard planted in Eden Valley (1847). Lower abv (11.5%) and softer natural acidity than neighbouring Clare reflects Eden Valley's higher-elevation, cooler-but-rounder profile vs Clare's steely austerity. Use as the regional-tier Australian Riesling reference (vs Grosset Polish Hill iconic tier), or as the price/quality contrast in a Riesling sweep, or as a Clare-vs-Eden intra-Australian sub-region trap. Selected over Eroica/Henschke Julius because Pewsey Vale publishes a full importer tech sheet PDF with verified analytical numbers.

entries.vintage_guidance: Current/recent vintage; 2022-2023 widely available. 2023 reviewed as 93pt JS — fresh, precise, restrained pyrazine.
entries.selection_notes: Classic biodynamic Sancerre estate blend from all three local terroirs (silex, caillottes, terres blanches). Useful as the limestone/marl-driven Sancerre anchor against a flint-driven Pouilly-Fumé like Dagueneau Silex, or against a NZ Marlborough Sauvignon for F1 same-variety origin spreads.

entries.vintage_guidance: Allocation-tier across recent vintages; 2019/2020/2022 critically lauded. Use current release; expect long aging window.
entries.selection_notes: Iconic Saint-Andelain silex-soil Pouilly-Fumé from old vines fermented partly in new 500/600L oak — the world reference for flint-driven, oak-aged Sauvignon Blanc. Critical foil to Sancerre (silex vs caillottes/marl, oak vs tank) and to NZ Sauvignon (savoury and structural vs fruit-driven).

entries.vintage_guidance: 2022/2023 currently in market; 2023 rated JD 96, Wine Spectator 93. Long-aging wine.
entries.selection_notes: Reference dry Vouvray from a single Première Côte vineyard (Le Mont, green-tinged clay over tuffeau). Defines the steely, flinty, age-worthy dry Chenin style — direct foil to Savennières (harder schist/volcanic terroir) and to SA Chenin (riper fruit profile). High cooldown weight because Huet/Vouvray/Foreau dominate Loire Chenin teaching examples.

entries.vintage_guidance: Current release typically 2020-2022; 2022 widely listed. Long aging potential; built for cellar.
entries.classification: Clos du Papillon — historic coulée, near-monopole (shared with Baumard)
entries.selection_notes: Benchmark Savennières from one of the three grand-cru-level coulées (Clos du Papillon, with La Coulée de Serrant and La Roche aux Moines). Schist/volcanic soils give a more savoury, structured Chenin than Vouvray — ideal F2 contrast inside Loire Chenin and useful as the high-intensity Old World Chenin in same-variety international flights.

entries.vintage_guidance: 2023 (14% ABV) and 2024 (13.5%, RS 1.8 g/l, TA 6.1 g/l, pH 3.22) currently in market. Distinct from the Mullineux Old Vines White blend already in the bank.
entries.selection_notes: Different cuvée from the already-banked Mullineux Old Vines White (which is a blend with Clairette/Sémillon/Viognier/Verdelho); this is a single-vineyard, single-variety Chenin from 47-year-old dry-farmed bush vines on decomposed granite, fermented in neutral French oak. Cleanest F1 'Loire vs SA Chenin' foil — flintier and more linear than Iron/Schist cuvées, so it pairs closely against Vouvray Sec stylistically.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2019 (a celebrated classic Barolo vintage) was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Benchmark village-level Barolo from a top traditionalist family producer; assembled from three high-altitude sites (Fossati, La Volta, Coste di Vergne) in Vergne. Use as the Nebbiolo anchor against Barbaresco for same-variety/different-zone flights, or against a single-cru Barolo for an origin quality-ladder question. Pale garnet, high acid + high gripping tannin, tar/rose/red cherry profile, neutral large-cask oak.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2022 is the current release researched.
entries.selection_notes: The single most consistent benchmark of value Nebbiolo: a co-op assembled from ~50 grower-members and 120 ha across the Barbaresco DOCG, with 24 days on skins and ~24 months in large botti. Use as the natural Barbaresco partner against a village Barolo for a same-variety/cross-zone flight (Barolo more powerful and tannic, Barbaresco more perfumed and earlier-drinking), or as the entry tier in a Produttori cru ladder.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2019 (an excellent Brunello vintage) was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Reference-standard Brunello from one of Montalcino's largest historic estates (Franceschi family, est. 1890s), built on the oldest estate vines between 150–450 m. 100% Sangiovese, submerged-cap fermentation, long aging in large French casks. Use as the Tuscan Sangiovese pinnacle against Chianti Classico Riserva and Vino Nobile in a same-variety/cross-zone flight, or as the upper rung of a Tuscan Sangiovese quality ladder.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020 was researched.
entries.selection_notes: Benchmark single-vineyard Chianti Classico Riserva from the high (400–420 m) Castelnuovo Berardenga zone on alberese-and-galestro soils. 100% Sangiovese, French-barrique aged 16–20 months. Use as the Chianti Classico anchor in a Tuscan Sangiovese same-variety flight (vs Brunello + Vino Nobile), or as the cru-Riserva rung above a straight Chianti Classico annata in a quality-ladder question. The 100% Sangiovese composition contrasts cleanly with Boscarelli Vino Nobile's traditional Sangiovese-plus-native blend.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020/2022 are current releases researched.
entries.selection_notes: Traditional Vino Nobile from a benchmark 1962-founded estate at Cervognano. 85% Sangiovese (Prugnolo Gentile) with the historic blend of Colorino, Canaiolo, Mammolo — preserves the older Tuscan Sangiovese-plus-natives style that diverges from the 100%-Sangiovese Brunello and Felsina Rancia. Slavonian + French Allier botti aging 18–24 months. Use as the third Sangiovese reference in a Tuscan cross-zone flight (Brunello / Chianti Classico / Vino Nobile) where the candidate must distinguish appellations of the same variety, or as the breadth wine in a mixed Tuscan reds question.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020/2021 researched.
entries.sub_region: Mount Etna (northern slope, Randazzo/Castiglione di Sicilia)
entries.selection_notes: Reference Etna Rosso from Marco de Grazia's pioneer estate on the volcano's northern slope (600–900 m, partly pre-phylloxera). 95–98% Nerello Mascalese + 2–5% Nerello Cappuccio, large-format French oak 16 months. Plays the F5 outlier role: pale, high-acid, savory volcanic red that frequently gets mistaken for Pinot Noir or Nebbiolo in blind exam contexts — a classic curveball wine, increasingly common in modern MW Paper 2 / Paper 3 questions. Use as the Sicily/Etna anchor in a 'mediterranean island reds' or 'identify the variety' flight.

entries.wine_name: Côte-Rôtie Brune et Blonde de Guigal
entries.vintage_guidance: Released annually with long élevage; 2019 widely available, 2020 in market. Signature classique négoce blend across Côte-Brune and Côte-Blonde lieux-dits.
entries.selection_notes: Reference Northern Rhône Syrah at benchmark quality and négoce house style. The 4% Viognier co-ferment, long 38-month élevage and partial new oak make this the textbook Côte-Rôtie style — useful as the Old World anchor in any cross-country Syrah/Shiraz flight (against Barossa, McLaren Vale, Hawke's Bay, Stellenbosch) and as the Côte-Rôtie reference inside a Northern Rhône quality ladder (Crozes → Saint-Joseph → Côte-Rôtie/Hermitage).

entries.vintage_guidance: 2020 and 2021 currently in market; both researched. Estate organic since 1950s and biodynamic since 1974, on 100 ha within the appellation at the northern limit exposed to the Mistral.
entries.selection_notes: Iconic Châteauneuf-du-Pape with the appellation's most Mourvèdre-led top-tier house style (regularly ~30% Mourvèdre vs Grenache-led peers). Anchors any Southern Rhône / GSM-style flight as the savoury, garrigue, structured benchmark to contrast against more Grenache-fruit-forward CdPs (Vieux Télégraphe, Pegau, Clos des Papes), Priorat blends, McLaren Vale GSM and Bandol. Long-aging foudre regimen rather than barrique gives a non-oaky structural profile useful for testing candidates on Mediterranean-blend recognition.

entries.vintage_guidance: Annual release; current vintage in market each spring. World's reference Provence rosé and #1-selling rosé in the US; LVMH Wines & Spirits since 2019.
entries.selection_notes: Category-defining Provence rosé — pale, dry, red-berry-and-citrus driven, stainless-steel-only, Grenache–Cinsault–Rolle led with Syrah/Tibouren accents. The MW Paper 3 anchor for any 'identify the rosé' question and the global commercial benchmark to contrast against saignée Tavel (deeper, GSM-heavy), Spanish rosado (Garnacha-led, fruitier), Loire Cabernet Franc rosé (red-fruit & herbal), and New World rosé. Use as the universal Provence rosé reference in a P3 mixed-style flight.

entries.vintage_guidance: 2021/2022 in market. Cuvée was renamed 'Lulu & Lucien' from the 2021 vintage onward in homage to founders Lucie (Lulu) and Lucien Peyraud, but the producer English page still references the 'Cuvée Classique' identity. Estate practising organic, biodynamic-influenced.
entries.selection_notes: Reference Bandol and the global benchmark for Mourvèdre-led Mediterranean reds. ~75% Mourvèdre, foudre-aged with no new oak, framed by sea-influenced limestone clay terroir — savoury, gripping, herbal-iodine, long-lived. Use as the Mourvèdre anchor in any 'identify the variety' P2 / P3 flight against Monastrell from Jumilla/Yecla (riper, plumper), Mataró-led California, and against other Provence reds (Bandol's iodine-leather signature is the discriminator). Pairs with Beaucastel CdP in a Mourvèdre-aware Southern French blend flight.

entries.vintage_guidance: Released annually; 2022/2023 in market. Foillard is a charter member of the 'Gang of Four' Beaujolais natural-wine pioneers (with Lapierre, Thévenet, Breton) under Jules Chauvet's tutelage; flagship cuvée from the famed Côte du Py decomposed-granite/schist hill of Villié-Morgon.
entries.selection_notes: Reference Cru Beaujolais and the textbook 'serious Gamay' style — whole-cluster, semi-carbonic, aged in neutral Burgundy barrels, no chaptalization or filtration. Foillard's Côte du Py is the canonical Pinot-Noir-adjacent red used in MW flights to test whether candidates over-jump to Burgundy: the iron-granite signature, brisker acid, and lighter tannin profile (vs Pinot's silkier tannin and cherry-stone earth) is the discriminator. Anchors the cru tier against village Beaujolais-Villages and basic Beaujolais in a quality-ladder flight.

entries.vintage_guidance: Annual release; current vintage in market. The category-defining mass-market Beaujolais-Villages — Duboeuf is nicknamed the 'King of Beaujolais' and is the world's largest commercial négociant-shipper of Beaujolais Nouveau and AOC Beaujolais. Sourced from 38 Villages-eligible communes via more than 300 contracted growers.
entries.selection_notes: Commercial Beaujolais-Villages floor for the Beaujolais quality ladder — light-bodied, low-tannin, fruit-forward Gamay with the textbook semi-carbonic banana/strawberry ester profile that betrays Beaujolais's carbonic identity. Useful as the value foil against Cru Beaujolais (Foillard Morgon) and against Pinot Noir / Loire Gamay in a 'name the variety AND quality tier' question. Standard global négociant style — appears in supermarket and bistro lists worldwide.

entries.vintage_guidance: Non-vintage; recent disgorgements based on the 2019/2021 vintages with 25% reserve wine. Researched batch: NV "White Foil" disgorged October 2024.
entries.selection_notes: Benchmark grande-marque NV — equal-thirds Pinot Noir/Meunier/Chardonnay, 25% reserve, 4 years on lees, 8–10 g/L dosage. Sits ABOVE the existing commercial-tier De Laurency NV foil in the bank and provides the proper NV-Champagne reference for any quality-ladder question (commercial NV → grande-marque NV → vintage prestige cuvée) and for cross-country traditional-method flights (vs Cava, Franciacorta, Nyetimber).

entries.vintage_guidance: Vintage prestige cuvée; only made in exceptional years. 2018 vintage researched (released 2025/2026). 2015 vintage also actively in market.
entries.sub_region: Aÿ / Montagne de Reims / Côte des Blancs
entries.classification: Vintage prestige cuvée — Grand Cru and Premier Cru parcels only
entries.selection_notes: Iconic vintage Champagne, Pinot-dominant, vinified entirely in old oak barriques and aged ~7 years on cork — the textbook NV-vs-vintage and stainless-vs-oak Champagne contrast in any prestige-tier flight. 2018: 66% PN / 34% Chard from 19 crus, ~73% Grand Cru / 27% Premier Cru, 7 g/L dosage. 2015: 60/40 PN/Chard, 11 crus, 8 g/L. Use against Pol Roger NV and Krug-style competition or to anchor the upper rung of a Champagne quality ladder.

entries.vintage_guidance: Single-vineyard, single-vintage, brut nature. Vintage releases lag heavily due to ~150-month minimum lees ageing. Current release: 2012 (ca. 1,561 numbered bottles). 2009/2011 also in market via verticals.
entries.appellation: Corpinnat (formerly Cava de Paraje Calificado prior to 2019)
entries.classification: Single-vineyard sparkling wine, brut nature, 0.97 ha estate plot planted 1940
entries.selection_notes: Spain's flagship terroir-driven traditional-method sparkling wine and the original Cava de Paraje Calificado before Recaredo's 2019 exit to Corpinnat. 100% Xarel·lo, 150+ months on lees, brut nature — the prestige Spanish counterweight to vintage Champagne in any traditional-method flight, and a curveball variety identification target (Xarel·lo can read as a long-lees Chardonnay/Champagne look-alike on the nose). Pairs with Pol Roger NV / Bollinger La Grande Année / Nyetimber Classic Cuvée for a four-country traditional-method spread.

entries.vintage_guidance: Multi-vintage flagship since 2016; current release blends multiple recent base years with ~25–35% reserve wines. Average ~30–36 months on lees before disgorgement.
entries.appellation: PDO Sussex / English Quality Sparkling Wine
entries.selection_notes: Reference English sparkling wine: same three Champagne varieties on Sussex chalk/greensand, 30+ months on lees, 12% ABV, brut dosage. The textbook "is it Champagne or English?" curveball — typically distinguishable by slightly higher acid, leaner fruit, and earlier release than vintage Champagne. Anchors the cross-country traditional-method flight alongside Pol Roger, Bollinger LGA, and Recaredo Turó d'en Mota.

entries.vintage_guidance: Non-vintage solera. Current bottlings draw from criaderas y solera maintained since 1844; minimum 4 years biological aging under flor.
entries.sub_region: Jerez de la Frontera (Macharnudo and Carrascal vineyards, Jerez Superior pago)
entries.classification: Fino — biologically aged Sherry, criaderas y solera system
entries.selection_notes: Globally iconic Fino — the calibration reference for biologically aged Sherry. Bone-dry, 15% fortification, ≥4 years under flor in albariza-grown Palomino. Use as the dry/biological end of a Jerez ladder (Fino → Amontillado → Oloroso → PX) or as a fortified-white curveball where candidates must distinguish flor character (saline, yeasty, almond, bitter) from oxidative aging (Lustau Don Nuño foil).

entries.vintage_guidance: Non-vintage solera. Average age in solera: ~12 years of fully oxidative aging. Bodega La Campana, Jerez de la Frontera.
entries.sub_region: Jerez de la Frontera (Bodega La Campana)
entries.selection_notes: Mid-tier dry Oloroso — fortified at 20% to suppress flor, ~12 years of pure oxidative aging in American oak. Palette anchor for the oxidative end of the Jerez ladder against Tío Pepe (biological flor) — the same Palomino base under two opposed aging regimes makes this the textbook MW exam Sherry-style discrimination pair. Useful in any fortified flight or as a dry / nutty / rancio curveball against PX, Cream Sherry, or aged Tawny Port.

entries.vintage_guidance: Non-vintage; blend of cask-aged tawnies with an average of 20 years in seasoned 630-litre oak casks at Taylor's Vila Nova de Gaia lodges. Continuously bottled to date of release.
entries.sub_region: Cima Corgo and Douro Superior (vineyards) / Vila Nova de Gaia (lodges)
entries.classification: Aged Tawny Port — 20 Years Old (Categoria de 20 Anos)
entries.selection_notes: Iconic 20-Year-Old Tawny — Taylor's reference style, the textbook aged-Tawny benchmark. Sweet (~111 g/L), 20% ABV, fully oxidative cask-aged blend showing dried fig, walnut, orange peel, and rancio. Producer chosen deliberately to diversify away from Quinta do Noval (cited in 2022 P3 Q3). Use as the oxidative-aged Port pole against a hypothetical reductive Vintage Port, or against Madeira (Bual) and aged dry Oloroso in a fortified flight where the candidate must distinguish sweet vs dry, oxidative vs biological, mutage vs flor.

entries.vintage_guidance: Non-vintage blend with average age of 10 years. Range was reformulated and rebottled in 2024 by winemaker Francisco Albuquerque using the Canteiro system across two ageing locations (Funchal Wine Lodge and Caniçal winery).
entries.sub_region: South coast of the island (Ribeira Brava to Calheta)
entries.classification: Aged Madeira — 10 Years Old, single grape variety (Bual / Boal), medium-rich
entries.selection_notes: Reference medium-rich Madeira — 100% Bual, 10 years Canteiro ageing, 19% ABV, ~95 g/L RS. Producer chosen to diversify away from Henriques & Henriques (heavily featured in 2021 P3 Q3). Sits in the middle of the Madeira sweetness ladder (Sercial → Verdelho → Bual → Malmsey) and provides the textbook "oxidative-but-not-rancio" benchmark — Madeira shares solera-like multi-decade ageing logic with Sherry/Port but adds the heat-and-time signature of the Atlantic island cellar. Pairs with Taylor 20yr Tawny and Lustau Don Nuño in any fortified-flight identification question.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2013, 2017 and 2018 widely circulating. Producer publishes per-vintage technical data: e.g. 2013 declared at 11% abv, 155.7 g/L residual sugar, 7.9 g/L total acidity; 2017 cited as a textbook botrytis year with three weeks of morning mists in October; 2018 record-breaking warm dry vintage with sporadic showers driving noble rot.
entries.sub_region: Mád / Mezőzombor (estate vineyards on volcanic and clay-loess soils)
entries.classification: Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos (legal minimum 120 g/L residual sugar under post-2013 PDO rules; on the label this is the second-highest Puttonyos classification below 6 Puttonyos and Aszú Eszencia)
entries.rs_level: ~150-160 g/L typical (155.7 g/L declared on 2013 vintage; minimum 120 g/L for 5 Puttonyos)
entries.selection_notes: Definitive Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos anchor for any P3 sweet flight. Royal Tokaji is the founding modern post-1990 producer (Hugh Johnson + investors). Use to differentiate Tokaji from Sauternes — note the Furmint + Hárslevelű blend, far higher acidity (7-9 g/L vs Sauternes ~4-5 g/L), botrytis berries macerated INTO fermenting base wine rather than vinified together, ageing in 300/500 L Hungarian oak fudders not Bordeaux 225 L barriques. Perfect foil to Coutet Barsac in a botrytis cross-country flight or alongside an Egon Müller BA in a sweetness-vs-mechanism trio.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; recent classic vintages include 2015, 2016, 2017 and 2019. Owned and managed by the Baly family; Baron Philippe de Rothschild S.A. holds exclusive worldwide distribution.
entries.sub_region: Sauternes-Barsac (commune of Barsac, between the Garonne and Ciron rivers)
entries.classification: Premier Cru Classé (1855 Bordeaux Classification, Sauternes-Barsac section — alongside Climens, Suduiraut, Rieussec, Coutet etc.; below the lone Premier Cru Supérieur d'Yquem)
entries.rs_level: sweet (Barsac Premier Cru style typically 110-150 g/L residual sugar)
entries.selection_notes: Mid-tier 1er Cru Classé Sauternes anchor — deliberately NOT d'Yquem or Guiraud (the two most-cited names) to add diversity. Coutet sits in Barsac sub-appellation (clay-limestone, knife-edge acidity hinted in the name 'couteau'), 75% Sémillon/23% Sauv Blanc/2% Muscadelle, 38-44 ha, 18 months in 70-100% new French oak, average 9 hL/ha yields, manual harvest in multiple tries. Use as: (i) the Bordeaux/Sauternes reference against a Tokaji Aszú or Mosel BA in a botrytis trio; (ii) part of a Sémillon-led spread (vs Hunter Valley Tyrrell's Vat 1); (iii) Barsac-specific question (linking with 1855 classification).

entries.vintage_guidance: Produced only in exceptional botrytis vintages and in tiny quantities. The 2024 release is the first Scharzhofberger Beerenauslese since 2015 — these wines are extreme rarities. Recent BA-producing years include 2003, 2005, 2007, 2010, 2015, 2024. Distributed exclusively through select merchants and the VDP Grosser Ring auction in Trier; estate does not sell direct.
entries.sub_region: Wiltingen / Saar — Scharzhofberg vineyard (devonian blue slate, south-facing, considered Grand Cru equivalent of the Saar)
entries.classification: VDP.Grosse Lage — Prädikat: Beerenauslese (legal Oechsle minimum ~110-128° depending on region; botrytis berry-by-berry selection)
entries.rs_level: sweet (BA band typically 150-220 g/L for Mosel BA; Egon Müller 2024 noted as lower-band BA-style but still classified as BA after acid-stopped fermentation)
entries.selection_notes: The apex of the Mosel Prädikat ladder — a true MW-exam curveball when paired with Tokaji and Sauternes in a botrytis trio. Note the contrast: Mosel BA is 100% Riesling, fermentation arrests naturally at 7-8% abv (vs ~13.5% Sauternes / ~11% Tokaji), residual sugar similar or higher, but acidity is the highest of the three (~9-11 g/L) — this acid-sugar tension is the Mosel signature. Deliberately distinct from the Dr Loosen and JJ Prüm entries already in the bank (Kabinett/Spätlese tier). Egon Müller produces these only in exceptional years; the 2024 is the first BA since 2015. Source confidence is medium because the estate does not publish a public BA tech sheet — sourcing relies on detailed Lobenberg retailer notes plus producer biography pages and JancisRobinson coverage.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 and 2025 currently in market. 2024 noted as exceptionally dry — the driest Marlborough season in 30+ years — slowing ripening and helping retain balance. Founded 1985 by David Hohnen of Cape Mentelle, now owned by LVMH.
entries.sub_region: Wairau Valley (Rapaura, Renwick, Brancott Valley sub-regions) — key blocks Estate, Motukawa, Widow's Block, Red Shed, Mustang
entries.selection_notes: The single most globally recognized Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc — founded 1985 by David Hohnen, now LVMH-owned, set the world standard for the Marlborough thiolic style. Mandatory anchor for any cross-country Sauv Blanc spread (vs Sancerre, vs Chilean Errazuriz already in bank, vs Pessac-Léognan, vs Marlborough Bordeaux blend). Wairau Valley gravelly free-draining soils, exceptional sunshine hours, marked diurnals → passionfruit/gooseberry/lime/white nectarine signature with sea-salt edge. Use to contrast with a flinty/reductive Sancerre or a textural Greywacke alternative; or as a commercial benchmark foil at the value end of an NZ flight.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2023 and 2024 widely available. Producer publishes per-vintage tech sheets with full analytical detail. 2024 reviewed by JancisRobinson team as 'slightly slimmer and more lithe' vs 2023. First vintage of this wine: 1994.
entries.sub_region: Blend across Lunlunta (Maipú, ~920 m), Gualtallary (Tupungato, ~1,450 m), and El Cepillo/La Consulta/Eugenio Bustos (Uco Valley, ~1,090 m)
entries.classification: Estate-tier cuvée (Catena's mainstream multi-vineyard blend; one tier above the regional Alamos line and below Catena Alta, Catena Zapata Malbec Argentino, and Adrianna Vineyard parcel wines)
entries.selection_notes: The mainstream/value Malbec anchor — deliberately the entry-level Catena Estate cuvée (~$15-25 retail) to create a price-and-quality ladder against the Catena Adrianna Vineyard River Malbec already in the bank (~$190-250). Same producer, same variety, same broad region, but the Estate blend smooths multiple altitudes (Lunlunta lowland + Gualtallary high + La Consulta mid) into a softer commercial expression vs Adrianna's single high-altitude parcel intensity. Excellent for: (i) Argentina price ladder questions; (ii) cross-country Malbec spreads (Catena vs Cahors Cosse-Maisonneuve / Lagrézette); (iii) Mendoza commercial anchor in any P2 reds flight. ID deliberately distinct from the existing Adrianna entry.

entries.vintage_guidance: Non-vintage / current-release blended NV style. World's #1 Vinho Verde by volume per producer; distributed in 70+ countries. Tech sheet publishes 80 hL/ha average yield, 6.5 g/L tartaric total acidity, pH 2.8-3.4, 11 g/L residual sugar.
entries.sub_region: Vinho Verde DOC (sourced across the region via grower partnerships into the Aveleda vinification centre)
entries.rs_level: 11 g/L (just off-dry in profile, technically dry by EU labelling)
entries.selection_notes: The default value white anchor — distinct from the Soalheiro Alvarinho premium entry already in the bank (which is single-variety, premium Monção). Casal Garcia is the world's #1-selling Vinho Verde, a multi-grape blend (Trajadura, Loureiro, Pedernã, Azal) at 9.5% abv, 11 g/L RS, slight petillance, ~$8 retail. Use as: (i) the commercial value-price floor in any P1 white flight; (ii) the cheap NV-style anchor in a Portugal flight vs Soalheiro / Niepoort; (iii) a low-alcohol curveball where the slight spritz and 9.5% abv directs candidates away from neutral varieties like Pinot Grigio; (iv) the unmistakable signal of granite-soils, multi-grape Vinho Verde — a classic IMW broad-commercial-anchor question.

entries.vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020, 2022, 2023 widely circulating. Producer publishes a tech sheet; 2020 importer (Vintus) PDF cites traditional long maceration in stainless steel with twice-daily pump-overs, and 24-30 months in neutral oak (this differs slightly from producer page which states 18 months in foudres — both descriptions are sourced; difference is between 'collection' bottlings vs vintage variation). ~4.5 million bottles produced annually.
entries.sub_region: Côtes du Rhône (broad regional sourcing across the southern Rhône; Guigal house in Ampuis, northern Rhône)
entries.selection_notes: The reference value red anchor — Guigal Côtes du Rhône Rouge is the most-shipped Rhône red in the world (~4.5 million bottles/year, retails ~$13-18). Deliberately Syrah-led (50% Syrah / 40% Grenache / 10% Mourvèdre) — atypical for a Southern Rhône blend which is more commonly Grenache-dominant — and aged 18-30 months in neutral oak / foudres, far longer than competitors at the price. This is a classic MW exam curveball: the structure says serious producer, the price band says value, the blend ratio whispers 'northern Rhône influence on a southern Rhône AOC.' Use as: (i) the value-end anchor in a P2 reds flight; (ii) Rhône AOC ladder question (CdR → CdR Villages → Gigondas → Châteauneuf); (iii) cross-country GSM/Syrah-blend comparison (vs South African Stellenbosch, vs Aussie Barossa GSM, vs Spanish Garnacha-led).

entries.vintage_guidance: Universally declared classic vintage — 2017 was only the second time in 144 years that consecutive years (2016 and 2017) were both universally declared. Field blend aged ~20 months in oak vats at Vila Nova de Gaia before bottling, then bottle-aged reductively for decades. Drinking window 2030+ per producer.
entries.sub_region: Cima Corgo and Douro Superior (Quinta de Vargellas, Quinta de Terra Feita, Quinta do Junco); aged at Vila Nova de Gaia lodges
entries.selection_notes: Iconic declared-vintage classic Port — the reductive bottle-aged pole to set against the already-banked Taylor Fladgate 20yr Tawny (same producer, opposite ageing regime). 2017 was a universally declared vintage (only 5 in the 21st century pre-2017), giving the candidate a textbook 'declared year' anchor. Field blend of Vargellas, Terra Feita, Junco; ~20 months in vat, ~100 g/L RS, 20% ABV. In a fortified flight, this is the high-tannin, deep-purple, structured-youthful Port that must be distinguished from aged Tawny (mahogany, rancio, dried fig) and from Madeira (Atlantic baked spice). Quinta do Noval deliberately avoided per 2022 P3 Q3 corpus appearance.

entries.vintage_guidance: Single-vintage 'cosecha' Gran Reserva bottling. Pedro Ximénez grapes harvested mid-August 1999, sun-dried on esparto mats for 7–9 days to concentrate sugars, pressed and fortified, then aged for over 20 years in American oak butts before bottling. The Gran Reserva tier carries a minimum 25-year oxidative ageing benchmark for Toro Albalá's Don PX line.
entries.sub_region: Aguilar de la Frontera (Córdoba); fruit from Sierra de Montilla and Moriles Altos superior pagos
entries.classification: Vintage 'Cosecha' Gran Reserva — sweet Pedro Ximénez, oxidatively aged
entries.selection_notes: Reference benchmark for sun-dried Pedro Ximénez — the extreme-sweet pole of the Andalusian fortified ladder. From Montilla-Moriles (where the bulk of PX historically came from; Toro Albalá was the first to bottle dessert PX themselves in 1970 rather than sell in bulk to Jerez bodegas), so it adds geographic and appellation diversity vs the existing Jerez entries (Tío Pepe Fino, Lustau Don Nuño Oloroso). At ~450 g/L RS, 17% ABV, and 20+ years in American oak butts, it provides the textbook 'molasses, fig, raisin, dark chocolate, coffee' PX profile any candidate must recognise on the P3 sweet axis. Producer chosen deliberately to diversify away from González Byass (already in bank as Tío Pepe) and Lustau (already in bank).

entries.vintage_guidance: Non-vintage 'Rare' tier solera blend — the top of the four-tier Rutherglen Muscat classification (Rutherglen → Classic → Grand → Rare). Average age 20+ years per the Muscat of Rutherglen Network's published ladder. Multiple decades of material blended by Madden Morris (6th generation Chief Winemaker). 165-year continuous family solera since 1859. Current bottling: Decanter Platinum 2024, IWC Australian Fortified Trophy + Muscat Trophy 2025, James Halliday 100 points.
entries.appellation: Rutherglen Muscat (Geographical Indication; Muscat of Rutherglen Network classification)
entries.classification: Rare Rutherglen Muscat — top tier (average 20+ years, multi-decade solera blend)
entries.selection_notes: Top-tier 'Rare' Rutherglen Muscat — the iconic Australian sweet-fortified anchor for any P3 flight. Made from Muscat à Petits Grains Rouges (Brown Muscat) grown in Rutherglen's continental North East Victoria climate, partially raisined on the vine, fortified mid-fermentation, then aged in a modified solera that draws on material spanning multiple Morris generations (winery est. 1859, currently 6th-gen Madden Morris). Provides the textbook 'olive-brown-with-green-rim, marmalade, christmas-pudding, rancio, integrated spirit' profile and lets the candidate place the wine on the four-step Rutherglen ladder. Strong curveball: shares some sensory DNA with aged Tawny Port and PX (dried fruit, rancio, sweetness) but the distinct marmalade/Christmas-cake/floral-Muscat lift is the key discriminator. Producer chosen for unrivalled medal record (Decanter Platinum 2024; IWC Australian Fortified Trophy + Muscat Trophy 2025; Halliday 100pts).
